name: Sora
tagline: OpenAI's AI video generation app shut down after burning $1M/day with declining users
description: Sora was OpenAI's text-to-video generation product launched in late 2024. Despite generating massive hype, the app burned through approximately $1 million per day in GPU compute costs, saw downloads drop from 3.3 million to 1.1 million in three months, and generated only $2.1 million in in-app purchases. OpenAI shut it down on March 24, 2026, discontinuing the app, website, and API.
story: Sora was supposed to be the product that proved AI could conquer video. OpenAI launched it in late 2024 with enormous fanfare — a text-to-video generator that could produce eerily realistic clips from simple prompts. Downloads surged past 3.3 million in November 2025. Then the numbers collapsed. By February 2026, monthly downloads had fallen to 1.1 million, and active users dipped below 500,000. The economics were brutal: Sora's diffusion transformer architecture required roughly 32x more compute per frame than competitors, with costs stuck at $1.40 per generated second — 16x higher than Kling's API pricing of $0.084. The app was burning through an estimated $1 million every single day. Regulatory pressure compounded the problem. The EU AI Act's synthetic media provisions required costly watermarking and content provenance infrastructure, reportedly costing OpenAI $40 million for Sora alone. Meanwhile, a planned partnership with Disney — which would have seen a $1 billion equity investment and licensing of 200+ characters — fell apart before any money changed hands. On March 24, 2026, CEO Sam Altman published 'Focusing Our Resources,' announcing Sora's immediate discontinuation. The web app would shut down April 26; the API would follow on September 24. OpenAI's most ambitious consumer product had lasted barely over a year, defeated by the economics of video generation and competition from cheaper alternatives like Kling, Runway, and Luma.
